summary: Pectoralis major has three heads of origin: clavicular, sternocostal, and abdominal, all inserting on the lateral lip of the bicipital groove.
Pectoralis major has two heads of origin: clavicular and sternocostal.
Pectoralis major inserts on the medial lip of the bicipital groove.
Pectoralis minor originates from ribs 3-5 and inserts on the coracoid process of the scapula.
Pectoralis minor inserts on the acromion process of the scapula.
Pectoralis minor originates from the clavicle.
The lateral pectoral nerve supplies the clavicular head of pectoralis major, while the medial pectoral nerve supplies pectoralis minor and the sternocostal head.
The medial pectoral nerve supplies the clavicular head.
Both pectoral nerves arise from the posterior cord of the brachial plexus.
The medial pectoral nerve pierces pectoralis minor before reaching pectoralis major — it is named 'medial' because it arises from the medial cord, not its position.
The medial pectoral nerve is named for its medial position in the chest wall.
The lateral pectoral nerve pierces pectoralis minor.
Approximately 75% of lymphatic drainage of the breast goes to the axillary lymph nodes, making them the most important group in breast cancer staging.
Most breast lymph drains to the supraclavicular nodes.
The axillary nodes receive only about 25% of breast lymph drainage.
The internal mammary (parasternal) lymph nodes receive drainage from the medial quadrants of the breast and are an important route of spread for medially located breast cancers.
The internal mammary nodes drain only the lateral quadrants.
Internal mammary nodes are not clinically significant in breast cancer.
